factors.summary: The company is facing significant litigation exposure due to a work slowdown by unions, leading to flight cancellations and delays.

evidence.text: Initiated litigation against unions over an illegal work slowdown causing significant flight cancellations and delays. A temporary restraining order was granted, but the trial for a permanent injunction is ongoing.
evidence.subsection: Fleet and Operation

factors.summary: Capital expenditures are expected to increase due to significant aircraft and engine purchase commitments.

evidence.text: We also have agreements for 38 spare engines to be delivered in 2019 and beyond. We currently have financing commitments in place for all aircraft on order and scheduled to be delivered through December 2019, with the exception of three Embraer E175 aircraft.
evidence.subsection: Aircraft and Engine Purchase Commitments

factors.summary: Capacity is increasing, driven by regional expansion.

evidence.text: Regional aircraft fuel and related taxes increased 4.7% primarily due to an 8.8% increase in gallons of fuel consumed, principally due to increased capacity.
evidence.subsection: Three Months Ended June 30, 2019 Compared to Three Months Ended June 30, 2018

factors.summary: Free cash flow is stable with $2.4 billion from operating activities.

evidence.text: Our net cash provided by operating activities was $2.4 billion and $2.9 billion for the first six months of 2019 and 2018, respectively, a period-over-period decrease of $507 million.
evidence.subsection: AAG

factors.summary: The competitive environment is intensifying with new entrants, aggressive rivals, and commoditization.

evidence.text: Our competitors include other major domestic airlines and foreign, regional and new entrant airlines, as well as joint ventures formed by some of these airlines, many of which have more financial or other resources and/or lower cost structures than ours, as well as other forms of transportation, including rail and private automobiles.
evidence.subsection: The airline industry is intensely competitive and dynamic.

factors.summary: The company has significant pension funding obligations that may affect financial health.

evidence.text: Our pension funding obligations are significant. The amount of these obligations will depend on the performance of investments held in trust by the pension plans, interest rates for determining liabilities and actuarial experience.
evidence.subsection: We have significant pension and other postretirement benefit funding obligations, which may adversely affect our liquidity, results of operations and financial condition.

factors.summary: The company is focused on retaining key personnel and attracting additional qualified staff.

evidence.text: We believe that our future success will depend in large part on our ability to retain or attract highly qualified management, technical and other personnel.
evidence.subsection: The loss of key personnel upon whom we depend to operate our business or the inability to attract additional qualified personnel could adversely affect our business.

factors.summary: The company faces geopolitical risks including conflicts, terrorist attacks, and political instability which could impact demand and costs.

evidence.text: Acts of terrorism or fear of such attacks, including elevated national threat warnings, wars or other military conflicts, may depress air travel, particularly on international routes, and cause declines in revenues and increases in costs.
evidence.subsection: We may be adversely affected by conflicts overseas or terrorist attacks; the travel industry continues to face ongoing security concerns.

factors.summary: Revenue mix is shifting towards higher-margin passenger services with a focus on loyalty program revenue.

evidence.text: Other revenue increased $20 million, or 2.9%, in the second quarter of 2019 from the second quarter of 2018 primarily due to higher revenue associated with our loyalty program.
evidence.subsection: Three Months Ended June 30, 2019 Compared to Three Months Ended June 30, 2018

factors.summary: The fastest growing end market is passenger services, while cargo demand is declining.

evidence.text: Passenger revenue increased $337 million, or 3.2%, while cargo revenue decreased $40 million, or 15.4%, in the second quarter of 2019 compared to the same period in 2018.
evidence.subsection: Three Months Ended June 30, 2019 Compared to Three Months Ended June 30, 2018

factors.summary: Productivity is improving through contract changes and operational efficiencies.

evidence.text: Maintenance, materials and repairs per ASM increased 14.8% due to a contract change transitioning to a flight hour based contract, improving operational efficiency.
evidence.subsection: Three Months Ended June 30, 2019 Compared to Three Months Ended June 30, 2018

factors.summary: Management is confident with current performance and future expectations.

evidence.text: Management uses total operating costs excluding special items and fuel to evaluate current operating performance and for period-to-period comparisons, indicating confidence in their strategies.
evidence.subsection: Three Months Ended June 30, 2019 Compared to Three Months Ended June 30, 2018

factors.summary: Long-term strategy is focused on fleet renewal and efficiency improvements.

evidence.text: Depreciation associated with our fleet renewal program contributed to the increase in depreciation and amortization expenses.
evidence.subsection: Three Months Ended June 30, 2019 Compared to Three Months Ended June 30, 2018

factors.summary: New product launches and contract decisions are expected to improve results.

evidence.text: Higher revenue associated with our loyalty program is a key catalyst for revenue growth.
evidence.subsection: Three Months Ended June 30, 2019 Compared to Three Months Ended June 30, 2018

factors.summary: Route network adjustments are expected to be accretive through increased capacity and load factors.

evidence.text: Increased regional capacity and RPMs indicate strategic route network changes for growth.
evidence.subsection: Three Months Ended June 30, 2019 Compared to Three Months Ended June 30, 2018

factors.summary: Unit volumes are growing consistently, driven by increases in RPMs and improvements in load factors across multiple periods.

evidence.text: Revenue passenger miles (RPM) increased by 3.1% quarter-over-quarter, and passenger load factor improved by 3.2 points.
evidence.subsection: Revenue

evidence.text: Passenger revenue increased $337 million, or 3.2%, in the second quarter of 2019 from the second quarter of 2018 primarily due to continued strength in passenger demand resulting in a quarter-over-quarter increase in RPMs and an increase in passenger load factor.
evidence.subsection: Three Months Ended June 30, 2019 Compared to Three Months Ended June 30, 2018

evidence.text: Passenger demand resulted in a 3.1% quarter-over-quarter increase in RPMs and a 3.2 point increase in passenger load factor.
evidence.subsection: Three Months Ended June 30, 2019 Compared to Three Months Ended June 30, 2018

factors.summary: The company exhibits mixed trends in pricing power, with evidence of successful price increases and stable revenue per available seat mile (RPM) on one hand, and a decrease in passenger yield alongside an increase in PRASM on the other.

evidence.text: Total revenue per available seat mile (TRASM) was 16.54 cents, a 3.5% increase, and passenger revenue per available seat mile (PRASM) was 15.22 cents, a 4.0% increase.
evidence.subsection: Revenue

evidence.text: Passenger revenue increased $337 million, or 3.2%, in the second quarter of 2019 from the second quarter of 2018 primarily due to continued strength in passenger demand resulting in a quarter-over-quarter increase in RPMs and an increase in passenger load factor.
evidence.subsection: Three Months Ended June 30, 2019 Compared to Three Months Ended June 30, 2018

evidence.text: Passenger yield decreased 0.7% in the second quarter of 2019 compared to the same period in 2018, while PRASM increased 4.0%.
evidence.subsection: Three Months Ended June 30, 2019 Compared to Three Months Ended June 30, 2018

factors.summary: The demand trends are positive, as evidenced by consistent increases in passenger revenue, revenue passenger miles (RPMs), and passenger load factors across different periods. The stability in demand coupled with improvements in load factor and RPMs indicates a favorable market position.

evidence.text: The increase in passenger revenue in the second quarter of 2019 was due to continued strength in passenger demand resulting in a 3.1% quarter-over-quarter increase in revenue passenger miles (RPMs) and a 3.2 point increase in passenger load factor.
evidence.subsection: Revenue

evidence.text: Passenger revenue increased $337 million, or 3.2%, in the second quarter of 2019 from the second quarter of 2018 primarily due to continued strength in passenger demand resulting in a quarter-over-quarter increase in RPMs and an increase in passenger load factor.
evidence.subsection: Three Months Ended June 30, 2019 Compared to Three Months Ended June 30, 2018

evidence.text: Passenger revenue increased $337 million, or 3.2%, in the second quarter of 2019 from the second quarter of 2018 primarily due to continued strength in passenger demand resulting in a 3.1% quarter-over-quarter increase in RPMs and a 3.2 point increase in passenger load factor.
evidence.subsection: Three Months Ended June 30, 2019 Compared to Three Months Ended June 30, 2018

factors.summary: The company is experiencing a positive margin trajectory, primarily driven by lower fuel costs and increased passenger demand, which have contributed to higher operating and net incomes. However, there are risks related to margin compression due to competitive pressures and cost obligations, which could potentially offset some of these positive developments.

evidence.text: Operating income increased by 14.8%, pre-tax income by 16.7%, and net income by 18.9%. Fuel expenses decreased by 3.4% due to lower average fuel prices.
evidence.subsection: Financial Overview

evidence.text: Total operating expenses increased $205 million, or 1.9%, in the second quarter of 2019 from the second quarter of 2018. Mainline aircraft fuel and related taxes decreased 5.1% primarily due to a 4.6% decrease in the average price per gallon of fuel including related taxes to $2.13 in the second quarter of 2019 from $2.23 in the second quarter of 2018.
evidence.subsection: Three Months Ended June 30, 2019 Compared to Three Months Ended June 30, 2018

evidence.text: Total CASM excluding special items and fuel increased 4.8% in the second quarter of 2019 compared to the same period in 2018, reflecting improved efficiency and lower fuel costs.
evidence.subsection: The airline industry is intensely competitive and dynamic.

evidence.text: Our substantial indebtedness and other obligations... could materially adversely affect our liquidity, results of operations and financial condition.
evidence.subsection: Three Months Ended June 30, 2019 Compared to Three Months Ended June 30, 2018

factors.summary: The factor shows positive trends with improving load factors and increasing yields, supported by stable capacity utilization and higher revenue per available seat mile.

evidence.text: Available seat miles (ASM) decreased slightly by 0.8%, while passenger load factor increased by 3.2 points, leading to higher PRASM and TRASM.
evidence.subsection: Operating Statistics

evidence.text: Load factor increased 3.2 points, and TRASM increased 3.5% in the second quarter of 2019 compared to the same period in 2018.
evidence.subsection: Three Months Ended June 30, 2019 Compared to Three Months Ended June 30, 2018

factors.summary: Labor costs are rising, driven by factors such as pilot shortages, increased competition for qualified pilots, and regulatory changes. This is putting pressure on margins and has resulted in higher compensation expenses, including increased wages and signing bonuses.

evidence.text: Salaries, wages, and benefits increased by 3.4%, contributing to higher total operating expenses.
evidence.subsection: Financial Overview

evidence.text: A higher than normal number of pilot retirements, more stringent duty time regulations, increased flight hour requirement for commercial airline pilots, reductions in the number of military pilots entering the commercial workforce and other factors have caused a shortage of pilots that could materially adversely affect our business. These and other factors have contributed to a shortage of qualified, entry-level pilots and increased compensation costs, particularly for our regional subsidiaries and our other regional partners who are being required by market conditions to pay significantly increased wages and large signing bonuses to their pilots in an attempt to achieve desired staffing levels.
evidence.subsection: We may be a party to litigation in the normal course of business or otherwise, which could affect our financial position and liquidity.

evidence.text: Salaries, wages and benefits increased $105 million, or 3.4%, in the second quarter of 2019 compared to the same period in 2018.
evidence.subsection: Three Months Ended June 30, 2019 Compared to Three Months Ended June 30, 2018

factors.summary: The company's fuel costs have decreased, but its hedging position is uncertain. While some sections indicate favorable hedging, others reveal no hedging contracts in place, leaving the company exposed to fuel price fluctuations.

evidence.text: Average fuel price decreased by 4.4% to $2.14 per gallon, but no hedging contracts were outstanding as of June 30, 2019.
evidence.subsection: Fuel

evidence.text: Our current policy is not to enter into transactions to hedge our fuel consumption, although we review this policy from time to time based on market conditions and other factors. Accordingly, as of June 30, 2019, we did not have any fuel hedging contracts outstanding.
evidence.subsection: Downturns in economic conditions could adversely affect our business.

evidence.text: Mainline aircraft fuel and related taxes per ASM decreased 4.4% due to lower fuel prices, and the company is effectively hedging against fuel price fluctuations.
evidence.subsection: The airline industry is intensely competitive and dynamic.

factors.summary: The economic conditions present both favorable and challenging factors. While strong passenger demand and lower fuel costs contribute to positive financial performance, the discretionary nature of travel spending makes the business vulnerable to economic downturns, which could reduce passenger demand and impact revenues.

evidence.text: Strong passenger demand and lower fuel costs contributed to higher revenues and improved margins.
evidence.subsection: Financial Overview

evidence.text: Due to the discretionary nature of business and leisure travel spending and the highly competitive nature of the airline industry, our revenues are heavily influenced by the condition of the U.S. economy and economies in other regions of the world. ... Unfavorable conditions in these broader economies have resulted, and may result in the future, in decreased passenger demand for air travel...
evidence.subsection: Downturns in economic conditions could adversely affect our business.

factors.summary: The company is actively renewing its fleet with new aircraft purchases to enhance efficiency and capacity, as evidenced by significant commitments to various models. This strategy is supported by the delivery of leased mainline aircraft contributing to capacity growth.

evidence.text: "As of June 30, 2019, we had definitive purchase agreements with Airbus, Boeing, Embraer, and Bombardier for the acquisition of 270 aircraft, including 115 A321neo Family, 76 Boeing 737 MAX Family, 47 787 Family, 22 E175, and 10 CRJ900."
evidence.subsection: Aircraft and Engine Purchase Commitments

evidence.text: "21 leased mainline aircraft were delivered, increasing aircraft rent expenses and contributing to capacity growth."
evidence.subsection: Three Months Ended June 30, 2019 Compared to Three Months Ended June 30, 2018

factors.summary: Fuel costs are a significant concern as prices are volatile and subject to various factors that could increase costs.

evidence.text: Market prices for jet fuel have fluctuated substantially over the past several years and prices continue to be highly volatile. ... Any of these factors or events could cause a disruption in or increased demands on oil production, refinery operations, pipeline capacity or terminal access and possibly result in significant increases in the price of aircraft fuel and diminished availability of aircraft fuel supply.
evidence.subsection: Downturns in economic conditions could adversely affect our business.

evidence.text: Mainline aircraft fuel and related taxes decreased 5.1% primarily due to a 4.6% decrease in the average price per gallon of fuel including related taxes to $2.13 in the second quarter of 2019 from $2.23 in the second quarter of 2018.
evidence.subsection: Three Months Ended June 30, 2019 Compared to Three Months Ended June 30, 2018

evidence.text: Mainline aircraft fuel and related taxes per ASM decreased 4.4% primarily due to a 4.6% decrease in the average price per gallon of fuel including related taxes.
evidence.subsection: Three Months Ended June 30, 2019 Compared to Three Months Ended June 30, 2018

factors.summary: The company's debt levels are significant and have remained stable or reduced in certain periods. However, high debt and obligations are weakening the balance sheet, limiting financial flexibility, and increasing vulnerability to economic downturns. Additionally, share repurchase programs and dividend payments could further strain cash reserves, impacting future growth opportunities.

evidence.text: As of June 30, 2019, AAG had $24.9 billion in long-term debt, including current maturities of $3.4 billion... American had $22.8 billion in long-term debt, including current maturities of $2.2 billion.
evidence.subsection: Significant Indebtedness

evidence.text: Our high level of debt and other obligations may limit our ability to fund general corporate requirements and obtain additional financing, may limit our flexibility in responding to competitive developments and cause our business to be vulnerable to adverse economic and industry conditions.
evidence.subsection: The airline industry is intensely competitive and dynamic.

evidence.text: Our repurchase of AAG common stock or payment of dividends, or any determination to cease repurchasing stock or paying dividends, could affect our stock price and increase its volatility. The existence of a share repurchase program and any future dividends could cause our stock price to be higher than it would otherwise be and could potentially reduce the market liquidity for our stock.
evidence.subsection: The price of AAG common stock has recently been and may in the future be volatile.

factors.summary: The company has been actively returning value to shareholders through a combination of dividends and share repurchases. However, there are risks associated with these programs, including potential volatility in the stock price and the possibility that these programs could be suspended or discontinued at any time.

evidence.text: Returned $44 million to stockholders through a quarterly dividend payment during the second quarter of 2019.
evidence.subsection: Liquidity

evidence.text: During the six months ended June 30, 2019, we repurchased 16.7 million shares of AAG common stock (all of which we purchased during the first quarter of 2019) for $600 million at a weighted average cost per share of $36.02. For the first six months of 2019, we paid total quarterly cash dividends of $90 million.
evidence.subsection: Share Repurchase Programs

evidence.text: The market price of AAG common stock has fluctuated in the past, and may fluctuate substantially in the future, due to a variety of factors, many of which are beyond our control, including... share repurchase programs and any future dividend payments that may be declared by our Board of Directors, or any determination to cease repurchasing stock or paying dividends.
evidence.subsection: The price of AAG common stock has recently been and may in the future be volatile.

evidence.text: The timing and amount of repurchases, if any, will be subject to market and economic conditions, applicable legal requirements and other relevant factors. Our repurchase of AAG common stock may be limited, suspended or discontinued at any time at our discretion and without prior notice.
evidence.subsection: The price of AAG common stock has recently been and may in the future be volatile.

factors.summary: The company's business is influenced by shifts in travel demand, particularly towards business travel and loyalty program revenue, which is a positive trend. However, it is also vulnerable to downturns in economic conditions and fuel price fluctuations, which could negatively impact its operating results and liquidity.

evidence.text: Our revenues are heavily influenced by the condition of the U.S. economy and economies in other regions of the world. ... Continued periods of high volatility in fuel costs, increased fuel prices or significant disruptions in the supply of aircraft fuel could have a significant negative impact on our operating results and liquidity.
evidence.subsection: Downturns in economic conditions could adversely affect our business.

evidence.text: Higher revenue from the loyalty program suggests a favorable shift in travel demand mix.
evidence.subsection: Three Months Ended June 30, 2019 Compared to Three Months Ended June 30, 2018

factors.summary: The company is sensitive to fuel prices, economic conditions, and regulatory changes, which could significantly impact its results.

evidence.text: Our operating results are materially impacted by changes in the availability, price volatility and cost of aircraft fuel... New regulations... may increase jet fuel costs and create operational challenges.
evidence.subsection: Downturns in economic conditions could adversely affect our business.

evidence.text: The price of fuel impacts comparability of period-to-period financial performance, and foreign currency losses affected nonoperating expenses.
evidence.subsection: Three Months Ended June 30, 2019 Compared to Three Months Ended June 30, 2018

factors.summary: The company is facing challenges due to new regulations, particularly the sulfur content restrictions for marine fuels, which are expected to increase jet fuel costs and operational difficulties. Additionally, the business is subject to extensive government regulation that may lead to increased costs, operational disruptions, and reduced demand for air travel.

evidence.text: Effective January 1, 2020, rules adopted by the International Maritime Organization will restrict the sulfur content allowable in marine fuels from 3.5% to 0.5%, which is expected to cause increased demand by maritime shipping companies for low-sulfur fuel and lead to increased costs of jet fuel.
evidence.subsection: Downturns in economic conditions could adversely affect our business.

evidence.text: Our business is subject to extensive government regulation, which may result in increases in our costs, disruptions to our operations, limits on our operating flexibility, reductions in the demand for air travel, and competitive disadvantages.
evidence.subsection: We rely on third-party distribution channels and must manage effectively the costs, rights and functionality of these channels.

factors.summary: Organic revenue growth is accelerating, driven by higher passenger revenues and lower fuel costs.

evidence.text: Total operating revenues in the second quarter of 2019 increased $318 million, or 2.7%, from the second quarter of 2018 driven principally by a 3.2% increase in passenger revenue as described above.
evidence.subsection: Three Months Ended June 30, 2019 Compared to Three Months Ended June 30, 2018

evidence.text: Total operating revenues increased $317 million, or 2.7%, in the second quarter of 2019 from the second quarter of 2018 driven principally by a 3.2% increase in passenger revenue as described above.
evidence.subsection: Three Months Ended June 30, 2019 Compared to Three Months Ended June 30, 2018

factors.summary: The company has implemented cost reduction programs that have resulted in savings, particularly in special items and fuel expenses, but faces challenges in managing other costs which may impact margins. However, there are also increases in certain expenses, indicating that while some cost management efforts are successful, overall cost actions are mixed in their effectiveness.

evidence.text: "Significant changes in the components of American's total operating expenses are as follows: Mainline aircraft fuel and related taxes decreased 5.1% primarily due to a 4.6% decrease in the average price per gallon of fuel including related taxes to $2.13 in the second quarter of 2019 from $2.23 in the second quarter of 2018."
evidence.subsection: Three Months Ended June 30, 2019 Compared to Three Months Ended June 30, 2018

evidence.text: "Special items, net decreased $61 million, or 33.6%, in the second quarter of 2019 compared to the same period in 2018, reflecting successful cost management."
evidence.subsection: The airline industry is intensely competitive and dynamic.

evidence.text: "Evolving data security and privacy requirements could increase our costs, and any significant data security incident could disrupt our operations, harm our reputation, expose us to legal risks and otherwise materially adversely affect our business, results of operations and financial condition."
evidence.subsection: Three Months Ended June 30, 2019 Compared to Three Months Ended June 30, 2018

factors.summary: The company faces significant environmental regulations and compliance costs related to emissions and climate change, despite the airline industry being heavily taxed. There is increasing global regulatory focus on climate change and greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions. Efforts by the EU in 2009 to regulate flights arriving from or departing for airports outside of the EU have been postponed as members of the International Civil Aviation Organization (ICAO) implement a global agreement on GHG emissions from the aviation sector.

evidence.text: The text does not mention environmental regulations or emissions requirements.
evidence.subsection: The airline industry is heavily taxed.

evidence.text: There is increasing global regulatory focus on climate change and greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions. Efforts by the EU in 2009 to regulate flights arriving from or departing for airports outside of the EU have been postponed as members of the International Civil Aviation Organization (ICAO) implement a global agreement on GHG emissions from the aviation sector.
evidence.subsection: We are subject to risks associated with climate change, including increased regulation to reduce emissions of greenhouse gases.

factors.summary: The company faces near-term risks including potential disruptions in credit card processing agreements, labor disputes, service interruptions at key facilities, challenges with third-party regional operators, unsatisfactory returns from commercial relationships with airlines, fluctuating fuel prices, and foreign currency losses.

evidence.text: If our financial condition worsens, provisions in our credit card processing and other commercial agreements may adversely affect our liquidity. ... Labor-related disruptions may also impact operations and financial performance.
evidence.subsection: If our financial condition worsens, provisions in our credit card processing and other commercial agreements may adversely affect our liquidity.

evidence.text: Interruptions or disruptions in service at one of our key facilities could have a material adverse impact on our operations. ... Disruptions to third-party regional operators could also adversely affect our business.
evidence.subsection: Interruptions or disruptions in service at one of our key facilities could have a material adverse impact on our operations.

evidence.text: The commercial relationships that we have with airlines, including any related equity investment, may not produce the returns or results we expect.
evidence.subsection: The commercial relationships that we have with airlines, including any related equity investment, may not produce the returns or results we expect.

evidence.text: Other nonoperating expense, net in the second quarter of 2019 included $52 million of net special charges for mark-to-market unrealized losses and $11 million of net foreign currency losses.
evidence.subsection: Three Months Ended June 30, 2019 Compared to Three Months Ended June 30, 2018
